description: Intra-domain (within-office) tool-selection golden set over BD-09 SD-09.1 Performance Measurement's near-duplicate return tools. Tool specs are authored faithfully from the SD-09.1 Service Operation descriptions (the oracle); the 4 focus tools are the confusable return calculations the SD spec itself distinguishes: time-weighted return, money-weighted return, strike-period-returns (sub-period linking), and gross-vs-net. The composite / currency / reconcile Service Operations are carried as additional catalogue tools so selection is realistic (not a closed 4-way), but the cases and labels centre on the 4 return tools, each appearing as both a correct answer and a confuser.

tools.name: Calculate time-weighted return
tools.description: Calculate the return that removes the effect of external cash flow timing. The basis for comparing a portfolio against a benchmark and against peers. Independent of when contributions and withdrawals occurred; geometrically links sub-period returns struck around each external cash flow so the figure measures the manager's decisions, not the investor's flow timing.

tools.name: Calculate money-weighted return
tools.description: Calculate the internal rate of return on the dated cash-flow series. The investor's actual experienced return wherever external cash-flow timing matters, and the standard performance basis in private markets. Solves for the single rate that discounts the dated contributions and withdrawals to the ending value; sensitive to the size and timing of the investor's cash flows (an IRR / dollar-weighted figure).

tools.name: Strike period returns
tools.description: Strike daily, monthly, quarterly, year-to-date and since-inception returns by linking the sub-period returns. Compounds the constituent single-period returns across a longer horizon to produce the period-end and cumulative figures for a chosen frequency; turns a series of short-interval returns into a YTD or since-inception number.

tools.name: Compute gross and net returns
tools.description: Compute return before and after management fees, performance fees and expenses. The gross figure is the return on the assets before any fee deduction; the net figure deducts management fees, performance fees and fund expenses to give the investor's after-fee return. The difference between the two is the total fee drag over the period.

tools.name: Compute composite returns
tools.description: Compute the asset-weighted return of a defined group of portfolios run to one strategy. Aggregates the member portfolios' returns weighted by their assets to produce the strategy-level composite figure used in GIPS presentation.

tools.name: Apply currency treatment
tools.description: Convert and, where a hedge applies, decompose local-currency, currency and hedged return. Separates the return earned in the asset's local currency from the currency-translation effect and from the contribution of any currency hedge.

tools.name: Reconcile return to the book of record
tools.description: Confirm the calculated return ties to the change in value and the cash flows recorded in the IBOR/ABOR. A control check that the produced return figure is consistent with the accounting book of record over the period.

cases.query: I need the portfolio return that strips out the timing of client contributions so I can compare the manager against the benchmark and peers.
cases.rationale: Removing external cash-flow timing for benchmark/peer comparison is the defining purpose of time-weighted return per SD-09.1. MWR is the trap (also a return over cash flows, but it does the opposite — it keeps flow timing in); period-linking and gross/net share the 'return' vocabulary.

cases.query: What return did the client actually experience given the size and dates of the money they put in and took out? I want the IRR on the cash-flow series.
cases.rationale: The investor's actual experienced return on the dated cash-flow series (IRR / dollar-weighted) is money-weighted return. TWR is the near-duplicate that deliberately removes exactly what MWR keeps.

cases.query: Take the monthly returns we already have and chain them into a year-to-date and since-inception figure.
cases.rationale: Linking sub-period returns into YTD / since-inception is the strike-period-returns operation. TWR is the confuser because TWR also links sub-period returns — but its job is the flow-timing removal, not the frequency compounding.

cases.query: Give me the return before fees and the return after deducting management and performance fees, so I can show the client the fee drag.
cases.rationale: Before-and-after-fee return and the fee drag is the gross-and-net operation. TWR/MWR are the confusers because either could be reported gross or net — but the fee-deduction transform itself is this tool.

cases.query: Produce a return that is neutral to when the investor added or withdrew cash, for the composite presentation.
cases.rationale: Flow-neutral return is TWR. 'composite' in the query is bait toward the composite tool; MWR is the flow-sensitive opposite.

cases.query: For the private-credit sleeve, compute the standard private-markets performance basis — the rate that discounts the drawdowns and distributions to the current value.
cases.rationale: The standard private-markets basis discounting dated cash flows is money-weighted return per SD-09.1. TWR is the public-markets near-duplicate.

cases.query: Compound our daily returns up to a clean quarterly and since-inception number for the fund.
cases.rationale: Compounding daily into quarterly/since-inception across a horizon is strike-period-returns. TWR shares the linking mechanism but is identified by flow removal.

cases.query: Show the after-fee return the investor keeps once management fees, performance fees and expenses come out, alongside the pre-fee figure.
cases.rationale: Pre-fee and after-fee return is gross-and-net. The 'investor keeps' phrasing is bait toward MWR (the investor's experienced return).

cases.query: I want the figure that reflects the manager's investment decisions only, with the effect of cash-flow timing taken out, to rank our managers.
cases.rationale: Manager-skill return with cash-flow timing removed is TWR. MWR is the flow-sensitive confuser.

cases.query: Compute the dollar-weighted return that accounts for how much money was invested at each point in time.
cases.rationale: Dollar-weighted, amount-and-timing-sensitive return is money-weighted return. TWR is the explicitly flow-insensitive near-duplicate.

cases.query: Chain the sub-period returns we computed into the cumulative figure at the chosen reporting frequency.
cases.rationale: Chaining sub-period returns into a cumulative frequency figure is strike-period-returns. TWR also chains sub-period returns — the deliberate confuser.

cases.query: What is the impact of fund expenses and fees on the return — the gap between what the assets earned and what the investor received after costs?
cases.rationale: The earned-vs-received-after-costs gap is the gross-to-net fee drag. The flow-return tools are the vocabulary confusers.

cases.query: Give me the time-weighted return for the mandate, not the money-weighted one.
cases.rationale: Names TWR explicitly while naming MWR as the rejected near-duplicate — the most direct adversarial pairing of the two confusable returns.

cases.query: Give me the money-weighted (IRR) return for this account, not the time-weighted figure.
cases.rationale: The mirror of C13: names MWR explicitly and TWR as the rejected confuser.

cases.query: Link our quarterly returns up to a since-inception number for the report.
cases.rationale: Linking quarterly into since-inception is strike-period-returns. TWR is the linking-mechanism confuser; composite is the report-context bait.

cases.query: Report both the pre-fee and the post-fee return so we can disclose the management fee and performance fee deduction.
cases.rationale: Pre-fee and post-fee return with the fee deduction is gross-and-net. The return-family confusers share most vocabulary.
